company.dd:
We are Uber. The go-getters. The kind of people who are relentless about our mission to help people go anywhere and get anything and earn their way. Movement is what we power. It’s our lifeblood. It runs through our veins. It’s what gets us out of bed each morning. It pushes us to constantly reimagine how we can move better. For you. For all the places you want to go. For all the things you want to get. For all the ways you want to earn. Across the entire world. In real time. At the incredible speed of now.

The idea for Uber was born on a snowy night in Paris in 2008, and ever since then our DNA of reimagination and reinvention carries on. We’ve grown into a global platform powering flexible earnings and the movement of people and things in ever expanding ways. We’ve gone from connecting rides on 4 wheels to 2 wheels to 18-wheel freight deliveries. From takeout meals to daily essentials to prescription drugs to just about anything you need at any time and earning your way. From drivers with background checks to real-time verification, safety is a top priority every single day. At Uber, the pursuit of reimagination is never finished, never stops, and is always just beginning.

content.backgroundSummary: I recently appeared for an interview assessment targeted at more experienced engineering roles.
content.generalNotes: I successfully solved both problems within the allotted time. The assessment was conducted under strict proctoring and emphasized efficiency and handling large constraints.
content.dl: 1884. Uber Interview Experience for Software Development Engineer 2

content.generalNotes: All of the rounds were conducted on an online coding assessment platform. I was provided with a practice link prior to the interviews, which I used extensively to get comfortable with the environment. My final verdict was a selection.
content.preparationStrategy: I spent two days prior to my managerial round creating a detailed 'Work Document' that mapped out my past projects using the STAR method. This allowed me to speak confidently about my achievements.

content.orgDescription: I interviewed for a role focused on expanding the city infrastructure to support faster dispatching and communication between hubs.
content.generalNotes: The interview was an online coding assessment which served as a disqualification round. It lasted for about 65 minutes and featured three technical problems.

content.generalNotes: I felt the interview process was rigorous but provided an amazing learning experience. Even though I received strong feedback in the algorithmic and behavioral rounds, the low score in the low-level design round was the deciding factor. It's a reminder that even for entry-level roles, depth in object-oriented design and machine coding is highly valued.
content.preparationStrategy: I focused on asking clarifying questions before jumping into code to show my thought process. I made it a point to think out loud and explain my reasoning for choosing specific algorithms, like BFS over DFS for shortest paths. I always started with a working solution before optimizing and remained mindful of time and space complexity throughout.
content.feedbackFromCompany: I received 'Strong Hire' ratings for the Data Structures & Algorithms and Hiring Manager rounds, but a 'Low Hire' for the Low-Level Design round, which resulted in the final rejection.
content.dl: 1789. Uber Interview Experience for Software Development Engineer 1

content.backgroundSummary: I applied for the role through a LinkedIn referral. A recruiter reached out shortly after and scheduled the initial technical assessment.
content.generalNotes: The interview was conducted by a Senior SDE. It was a standard technical discussion followed by a deep dive into coding and optimization. I felt comfortable with the initial problem but found the optimization follow-up challenging within the allotted time.

content.backgroundSummary: I had attempted over 50 online assessments and gone through 5 technical interviews before securing this opportunity.
content.generalNotes: I learned that clarity of thought and communication are often valued more than perfect code implementation. Even with an incomplete or slightly buggy solution, demonstrating a correct approach and reasoning can lead to a positive result.
content.timelineResultWaitTime: A few days after the online assessment
content.preparationStrategy: I practiced extensively by attempting numerous online assessments to get used to different problem types and time constraints.

content.generalNotes: My experience was quite exhausting as my interviews were rescheduled about 5 to 6 times, which required me to constantly stay prepared. Despite the technical depth I provided during the design discussion, the feedback was focused on me not asking enough clarifying questions at the start. It felt like the bar was very high or they were looking for specific reasons to down-level candidates due to high competition.

content.orgDescription: I interviewed for a role focused on the core marketplace systems in the LATAM region.
content.backgroundSummary: I applied for an L4 position, which was my third attempt at joining the company.
content.generalNotes: This was my third attempt at an L4 role. The process included a specialization round followed by coding stages. I felt the interviewer's style in the coding round was unusual as they prioritized finding the most optimal optimization before I could establish a baseline code structure.
content.preparationStrategy: I studied extensively for this third attempt, focusing on algorithms and data structures relevant to logistics and grid-based problems.
